questions.stem_en: What is 35% of 240?

options.label: B

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Used only 30% of 240: 3 × 24 = 72, missing the extra 5%.
options.trap_reason_hi: सिर्फ 30% लिया: 3 × 24 = 72; extra 5% छूट गया।

questions.explanation_concept_en: 'Percent' means 'per 100'. p% of N = (p × N)/100. The fraction p/100 is the multiplier; multiplying any number N by it scales N down proportionally. 10% chunks are easy mental anchors — split the rate into 10s and remainders.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: 'percent' का मतलब 'per 100' है। p% of N = (p × N)/100। यह fraction p/100 multiplier है जो किसी भी संख्या N को proportional रूप से scale करता है। 10% के chunks सबसे आसान mental anchor हैं — rate को 10 और शेष में बाँटें।

questions.stem_en: If a number is increased by 20% and then decreased by 20%, what is the net change?
questions.stem_hi: किसी संख्या को 20% बढ़ाकर फिर 20% घटाया जाए, तो net change क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Common error — students subtract 20-20 = 0, but the bases differ. The 20% decrease is on the increased number (120%), not on the original.
options.trap_reason_hi: common गलती — 20-20 = 0 कर दिया, लेकिन base अलग हैं। 20% decrease बढ़ी हुई संख्या (120%) पर लगता है, मूल पर नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Right magnitude, wrong direction — successive +x% and −x% always result in a net decrease, never an increase.
options.trap_reason_hi: magnitude सही, दिशा गलत — successive +x% और −x% का net result हमेशा कमी होगा, बढ़त नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Incorrectly added the percentages. Successive percentages compound, they don't add.
options.trap_reason_hi: percentages को जोड़ दिया। successive percentages compound होते हैं, जोड़े नहीं जाते।

questions.explanation_method_en: Use the formula: net% = a + b + (a×b)/100, where a = +20, b = −20. Net = 20 − 20 + (20)(−20)/100 = 0 − 4 = −4%. So 4% decrease.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Take 100 as the original. After +20%: 120. After −20% on 120: 120 × 0.80 = 96. Final = 96, drop = 4 on 100 = 4%. The two ±x% adjustments don't cancel because the base of the second adjustment (120) is larger than the base of the first (100); the absolute decrease 24 exceeds the absolute increase 20.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: 100 को मूल मानें। +20% के बाद: 120। 120 पर −20% = 120 × 0.80 = 96। Final = 96, गिरावट = 100 में से 4 = 4%। ±x% adjustments cancel नहीं होते क्योंकि दूसरे adjustment का base (120) पहले के base (100) से बड़ा है; absolute कमी 24 absolute बढ़त 20 से ज़्यादा है।

questions.stem_en: A number when increased by 25% becomes 80. What was the original number?
questions.stem_hi: एक संख्या को 25% बढ़ाने पर 80 हो जाती है। मूल संख्या क्या थी?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 25 from 80 = 55, then approximated up. Wrong because 25% of original ≠ 25 absolute.
options.trap_reason_hi: 80 से 25 घटाकर 55 निकाला और round किया। गलत क्योंकि मूल का 25% absolute 25 नहीं है।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 25 directly from 80 — confusing 25% of original with 25 units.
options.trap_reason_hi: 80 में से 25 घटाया — मूल का 25% को 25 units समझा।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 25% from 80: 80 − 20 = 60, then over-corrected. The relationship is 80 = 1.25 × x, so x = 64.
options.trap_reason_hi: 80 से 20 घटाया, फिर over-correct कर दिया। सही: 80 = 1.25 × x, अतः x = 64।

questions.explanation_method_en: If +25% gives 80, then 80 represents 125% of original. Original = 80 × 100/125 = 80 × 0.8 = 64.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Reverse-percentage problems are about identifying which side of the comparison is the 'whole' (100%). Here 'after a 25% increase' means the result is 125% of the start. To recover the start, divide the result by the multiplier (1.25), not by adding/subtracting 25 to the result.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Reverse-percentage problems में पहचानना है कि comparison में 'whole' (100%) कौन है। यहाँ '25% बढ़ाने पर' का मतलब result मूल का 125% है। मूल को निकालने के लिए result को multiplier (1.25) से भाग दें — न कि result में 25 जोड़ें/घटाएँ।

questions.stem_en: 60% of a number is 144. What is 75% of the same number?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: This is the original number (60% × 240 = 144), not 75% of it.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Added 24 (15 increase ratio) to 144 — used absolute additive logic instead of ratio.
options.trap_reason_hi: 144 में 24 जोड़ा (15 increase ratio) — ratio की जगह additive logic लगाया।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Added 48 = 144 × 1/3, mistaking 75/60 for 4/3 instead of 5/4.

questions.explanation_method_en: Ratio shortcut: 75% / 60% = 75/60 = 5/4. So 75% = 144 × 5/4 = 180. No need to find the number first.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Both 60% and 75% are different fractions of the same whole. Their ratio (75/60 = 5/4) tells you directly how the second value relates to the first. This 'cross-percent' shortcut saves a step over finding the original number (240) and then 75% of it (180).
questions.explanation_concept_hi: 60% और 75% दोनों एक ही whole के अलग-अलग fractions हैं। उनका ratio (75/60 = 5/4) सीधे बताता है कि दूसरा value पहले से कितना है। यह 'cross-percent' shortcut मूल संख्या (240) निकालने और फिर 75% (180) निकालने का step बचाता है।

questions.stem_en: Ramesh spends 75% of his salary. If his salary is increased by 20% and his expenses by 10%, by what percent do his savings change?
questions.stem_hi: रमेश अपनी salary का 75% खर्च करता है। यदि उसकी salary 20% बढ़े और expenses 10% बढ़ें, तो savings कितने प्रतिशत बदलेंगी?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 10 from 20 — but salary and expenses are different bases, you can't compare percentages directly.
options.trap_reason_hi: 20 से 10 घटाया — पर salary और expenses के base अलग हैं, percentages को सीधे compare नहीं कर सकते।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Added 20 + 10 = 30 — wrong direction, expenses cut savings, they don't add.
options.trap_reason_hi: 20 + 10 = 30 जोड़ा — दिशा गलत, expenses savings कम करते हैं, जोड़ते नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Found savings increase = 12.5, then divided by 50 instead of original savings 25: 12.5/50 = 25%.
options.trap_reason_hi: savings increase = 12.5 निकाला, फिर original savings 25 की जगह 50 से divide किया: 12.5/50 = 25%।

questions.explanation_method_en: Take salary = 100, so expenses = 75 and savings = 25. New salary = 120, new expenses = 75 × 1.10 = 82.5. New savings = 120 − 82.5 = 37.5. Change = (37.5 − 25)/25 = 50%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Savings are the residual: salary minus expenses. When two different bases (here 100 and 75) grow at different rates, the residual's percent change depends on the absolute changes, not the percent changes. Always assume a clean base (e.g., 100), compute absolute amounts, and only then convert the change in the residual back to a percent.
questions.explanation_method_hi: salary = 100 मानें, expenses = 75, savings = 25। नई salary = 120, नई expenses = 75 × 1.10 = 82.5। नई savings = 120 − 82.5 = 37.5। Change = (37.5 − 25)/25 = 50%।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Savings residual हैं: salary − expenses। जब दो अलग bases (यहाँ 100 और 75) अलग rates पर बढ़ते हैं, तो residual का percent change percent changes पर नहीं, absolute changes पर निर्भर करता है। हमेशा clean base (e.g. 100) मान लें, absolute amounts निकालें, फिर residual के change को percent में बदलें।

questions.stem_en: If the price of sugar increases by 25%, by what percentage should a family reduce its consumption to keep the expenditure on sugar unchanged?
questions.stem_hi: यदि चीनी की कीमत 25% बढ़ जाए, तो परिवार को consumption कितने प्रतिशत घटानी चाहिए कि sugar पर expenditure same रहे?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Symmetric instinct — but cutting consumption by 25% only offsets a small price rise, not 25%. Bases differ.
options.trap_reason_hi: symmetric instinct से — पर 25% consumption cut छोटी price rise को offset करता है, 25% को नहीं। bases अलग।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used 25% price rise minus a 10-point adjustment to get 15%. But 1.25 × 0.85 = 1.0625, so expenditure still rises by 6.25%.
options.trap_reason_hi: 25% price rise में से 10-point adjustment घटाकर 15% लिया। पर 1.25 × 0.85 = 1.0625, इसलिए expenditure फिर भी 6.25% बढ़ता है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Overcompensated — 30% consumption cut would leave expenditure lower than original.
options.trap_reason_hi: Overcompensate — 30% consumption cut से expenditure मूल से कम हो जाता है।

questions.explanation_method_en: Formula: required reduction% = (rise%)/(100 + rise%) × 100 = 25/125 × 100 = 20%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Expenditure = price × consumption. To keep it constant when price multiplies by 1.25, consumption must multiply by 1/1.25 = 0.80, i.e., reduce by 20%. The asymmetry between 25% rise and 20% cut comes from the base inversion: rising 25% on the smaller base, cutting 20% on the larger base.
questions.explanation_method_hi: formula: needed cut% = (rise%)/(100 + rise%) × 100 = 25/125 × 100 = 20%।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Expenditure = price × consumption. इसे constant रखने के लिए जब price 1.25 गुना हो, तो consumption को 1/1.25 = 0.80 गुना करना होगा, यानी 20% कम। 25% बढ़त और 20% कमी की asymmetry base के बदलने से आती है: छोटे base पर 25% बढ़त, बड़े base पर 20% कमी।

questions.stem_en: In an election between two candidates, the loser polled 45% of total valid votes and lost by 4000 votes. How many total valid votes were cast?
questions.stem_hi: दो उम्मीदवारों के बीच चुनाव में हारने वाले को 45% valid votes मिले और वह 4000 votes से हारा। कुल valid votes कितने थे?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Used (4000/20%) but with wrong margin — 4000 = 10% of total, not 20%.
options.trap_reason_hi: (4000/20%) लगाया पर margin गलत — 4000 = total का 10%, 20% नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used 4000 ÷ 0.5 — confused half-margin shortcut with the percent gap.
options.trap_reason_hi: 4000 ÷ 0.5 लगाया — half-margin shortcut को percent gap समझ लिया।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed 4000/9% (treating 9% wrongly as the margin). Margin is 10%.
options.trap_reason_hi: 4000/9% लगाया (9% margin गलत)। सही margin 10% है।

questions.explanation_method_en: Loser got 45%, winner got 55%. Margin = 55% − 45% = 10% of total = 4000 votes. So total = 4000/0.10 = 40000.
questions.explanation_concept_en: When two parties split 100% and one has p%, the other has (100−p)%. The 'margin' is the absolute difference (100−2p)%. Convert any margin in absolute units (here 4000 votes) to total by dividing by the margin's fractional value.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: जब दो parties 100% बाँटें और एक को p% मिले, तो दूसरे को (100−p)%। margin absolute difference (100−2p)% होता है। किसी भी margin को absolute units (यहाँ 4000 votes) से total तक पहुँचाने के लिए margin के fractional value से भाग दें।

questions.stem_en: The population of a town increased from 20000 to 23000. Find the percentage increase.
questions.stem_hi: एक कस्बे की जनसंख्या 20000 से 23000 हो गई। percentage increase निकालिए।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Used (3000/23000)×100 — divided by the new value instead of the old.
options.trap_reason_hi: (3000/23000)×100 लगाया — नए value से भाग दिया, पुराने से नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed 3000/20000 = 0.15 but forgot to multiply by 100 to convert to %.

questions.explanation_concept_en: Percentage change is always relative to the OLD value (the base). Formula: %change = (new − old)/old × 100. Dividing by the new value (23000) gives the wrong answer because that's not the base of the increase — the increase happened from 20000.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Percentage change हमेशा OLD value (base) के सापेक्ष होता है। Formula: %change = (new − old)/old × 100। नई value (23000) से भाग देने पर answer गलत आता है क्योंकि वह base नहीं है — बढ़त 20000 से हुई है।

questions.stem_en: A's salary is 25% more than B's salary. By what percentage is B's salary less than A's?
questions.stem_hi: A की salary B की salary से 25% अधिक है। B की salary A की salary से कितने प्रतिशत कम है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Common error: assumed symmetry. But the bases differ — 25% of B (smaller) ≠ 25% of A (larger).
options.trap_reason_hi: common गलती: symmetry मान ली। पर bases अलग हैं — B (छोटा) का 25% ≠ A (बड़ा) का 25%।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Overcorrection — sensed asymmetry but went the wrong way. Less salary's percent gap is smaller, not bigger.
options.trap_reason_hi: Overcorrect — asymmetry पकड़ी पर दिशा गलत। कम salary का percent gap छोटा होता है, बड़ा नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Used 25 × 0.6 — wrong shortcut. Correct shortcut is 25/(100+25) × 100 = 20.

questions.explanation_method_en: Shortcut: B<A by (25/125) × 100 = 20%. Generally, if X is x% more than Y, Y is x/(100+x) × 100% less than X.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Take B = 100, then A = 125. Difference = 25. To express the difference as a percent of A: 25/125 = 20%. The reason 25% ≠ 20% is the base shift: when comparing 'A more than B' the base is B; when comparing 'B less than A' the base is A. Larger base means smaller percent.
questions.explanation_method_hi: shortcut: B<A by (25/125) × 100 = 20%। सामान्य: यदि X, Y से x% अधिक है, तो Y, X से x/(100+x) × 100% कम है।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: B = 100 मानें, A = 125। अंतर = 25। A के percent में: 25/125 = 20%। 25% ≠ 20% होने का कारण base shift है: 'A from B more' में base B है; 'B from A less' में base A। बड़ा base = छोटा percent।

questions.stem_en: An article is marked at ₹500. After a discount of 10% and an additional 5% off on the discounted price, what is the final price?
questions.stem_hi: एक वस्तु का marked price ₹500 है। पहले 10% discount, फिर discounted price पर 5% और discount। अंतिम कीमत क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 15% directly from ₹500 — but successive discounts compound, they don't add.
options.trap_reason_hi: ₹500 में से 15% सीधे घटाया — successive discounts compound होते हैं, जोड़े नहीं जाते।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Computed only the first discount and stopped.
options.trap_reason_hi: केवल पहला discount निकाला, दूसरा भूल गए।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Took only 5% off the marked price — applied wrong discount.
options.trap_reason_hi: marked price से सिर्फ 5% घटाया — wrong discount लगाया।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Successive percentage changes multiply, they don't add. Each later discount applies to the already-reduced price. Equivalent single rate for two successive discounts a%, b% is: 1 − (1−a/100)(1−b/100). Here = 1 − 0.90 × 0.95 = 0.145 = 14.5%, less than 10+5 = 15%.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Successive percentage changes multiply होते हैं, जुड़ते नहीं। हर अगला discount पहले से कम हुई price पर लगता है। दो successive discounts a%, b% का equivalent single rate: 1 − (1−a/100)(1−b/100)। यहाँ = 1 − 0.90 × 0.95 = 0.145 = 14.5%, जो 10+5 = 15% से कम है।

questions.stem_en: If 70% of students pass an exam and 24 students fail, how many students appeared?
questions.stem_hi: यदि 70% छात्र pass हों और 24 छात्र fail हों, तो कुल कितने छात्र appeared थे?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Used 24 ÷ 0.40 — used wrong fail percentage.
options.trap_reason_hi: 24 ÷ 0.40 लगाया — fail % गलत।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used 24/24 = 1, then converted it to 100; the fail fraction should be 30%, so total = 24/0.30 = 80.
options.trap_reason_hi: 24/24 = 1 करके 100 लिया; fail fraction 30% है, इसलिए total = 24/0.30 = 80।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Used 24 ÷ 0.20 — used 20% fail rate, but actual is 30%.

questions.explanation_method_en: Pass% = 70 ⇒ Fail% = 30. 30% of total = 24 ⇒ total = 24 ÷ 0.30 = 80.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Pass and fail are mutually exclusive and cover all candidates, so they sum to 100%. Whenever you have an absolute count tied to a percent, divide the count by the percent's fractional value (here 0.30, NOT 0.70 — because 24 is the count of failers, not passers).
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Pass और fail mutually exclusive हैं और सब को cover करते हैं, अतः sum 100%। जब absolute count किसी percent से जुड़ी हो, तो count को percent के fractional value से भाग दें (यहाँ 0.30, 0.70 नहीं — क्योंकि 24 fail हुए हैं, pass नहीं)।

questions.stem_en: A's income is 20% less than B's. B's income is 25% less than C's. A's income is what percent of C's?
questions.stem_hi: A की income B की income से 20% कम है। B की income C की income से 25% कम है। A की income C की income का कितने प्रतिशत है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 20+25 = 45% directly from 100% — wrong, compound effects.
options.trap_reason_hi: 20+25 = 45% सीधे घटाया — गलत, compound effect।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 20 and 25 from 100, then added back 10: 100 - 45 + 10 = 65. The overlap correction should be 5, so A is 60% of C.
options.trap_reason_hi: 100 से 20 और 25 घटाकर 10 वापस जोड़ा: 100 - 45 + 10 = 65। correction 5 होना चाहिए, इसलिए A = 60% of C।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Took only the B-vs-C step (75%) and forgot the A-vs-B step.
options.trap_reason_hi: केवल B-vs-C step (75%) लिया, A-vs-B भूले।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Chain percentages of the form 'X is x% less than Y, Y is y% less than Z' compose multiplicatively: A/C = (A/B)(B/C) = (1−x/100)(1−y/100). Adding x and y is wrong because each percent is on a different base in the chain.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: 'X, Y से x% कम है, Y, Z से y% कम है' इस chain में percentages गुणा से compose होते हैं: A/C = (A/B)(B/C) = (1−x/100)(1−y/100)। x + y जोड़ना गलत है क्योंकि chain में हर percent अलग base पर है।

questions.stem_en: In a class of 50 students, 60% are boys. If 8 girls are absent on a particular day, what percent of the class present is girls?
questions.stem_hi: 50 छात्रों की class में 60% लड़के हैं। एक दिन 8 लड़कियाँ absent हैं — present class में लड़कियाँ कितने प्रतिशत हैं?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Used 12/60 instead of 12/42 — divided present girls by total class, not present class.
options.trap_reason_hi: 12/60 लगाया, सही 12/42 — divisor present class नहीं, total लिया।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: This is the original girls% — but absent girls reduced it.
options.trap_reason_hi: मूल girls% यही है — पर absent ने इसे कम कर दिया।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Used 37.5 as the present-class base instead of 42: 12/37.5 × 100 = 32%.
options.trap_reason_hi: present class base 42 की जगह 37.5 लिया: 12/37.5 × 100 = 32%।

questions.explanation_method_en: Total = 50, boys = 30, girls = 20. Absent girls = 8 ⇒ present girls = 12. Present class = 50 − 8 = 42. % = 12/42 × 100 ≈ 28.57%. Rounded to one decimal, this is 28.6%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: When the population's composition changes (here through absences), recompute the relevant base. The 'percent present girls' takes 'present girls' as the numerator and 'present total' (not original total) as the denominator. The boys count stays at 30 because no boys are absent.
questions.explanation_method_hi: total = 50, लड़के = 30, लड़कियाँ = 20। absent लड़कियाँ = 8 ⇒ present लड़कियाँ = 12। present class = 50 − 8 = 42। % = 12/42 × 100 ≈ 28.57%। एक decimal तक round करने पर यह 28.6% है।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: जब population का composition बदले (यहाँ absences से), तो relevant base फिर से निकालें। 'percent present girls' का numerator 'present girls' और denominator 'present total' (मूल total नहीं) है। लड़के 30 वही रहे क्योंकि कोई लड़का absent नहीं।

questions.stem_en: The price of a commodity rose by 30%. The expenditure on it increased by only 8%. By what percentage did consumption decrease?
questions.stem_hi: एक commodity की कीमत 30% बढ़ी। उस पर expenditure सिर्फ 8% बढ़ा। consumption कितने प्रतिशत घटी?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 8 from 30 directly — but expenditure = price × consumption, ratios involved.
options.trap_reason_hi: 30 में से 8 घटाया — पर expenditure = price × consumption, ratios लगते हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used price ratio 1.35 instead of 1.30: 1.08/1.35 = 0.80, giving a 20% drop.
options.trap_reason_hi: price ratio 1.30 की जगह 1.35 लिया: 1.08/1.35 = 0.80, इसलिए 20% drop।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Cancelled the price rise entirely — would mean expenditure didn't change.
options.trap_reason_hi: price rise पूरा cancel कर दिया — पर तब expenditure वही रहती।

questions.explanation_method_en: expenditure = price × consumption. So consumption_ratio = expenditure_ratio / price_ratio = 1.08 / 1.30 ≈ 0.8308. Drop ≈ (1 − 0.8308) × 100 ≈ 16.9%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Expenditure is the product of price and consumption. If price rises by p% and expenditure rises by e%, the consumption multiplier is (1 + e/100)/(1 + p/100). The drop in consumption is whatever short-fall keeps expenditure from rising as much as the price did.
questions.explanation_method_hi: expenditure = price × consumption। तो consumption_ratio = expenditure_ratio / price_ratio = 1.08/1.30 ≈ 0.8308। drop ≈ (1 − 0.8308) × 100 ≈ 16.9%।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Expenditure = price × consumption। यदि price p% बढ़े और expenditure e% बढ़े, तो consumption multiplier = (1 + e/100)/(1 + p/100)। consumption में जितनी कमी आए, उतनी ही expenditure की rise को price-rise से कम रखती है।

questions.stem_en: A number is increased by 50% and then decreased by 50%. The net change is:
questions.stem_hi: किसी संख्या को 50% बढ़ाया जाए और फिर 50% घटाया जाए। net change है:

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Symmetry trap — 50−50 doesn't equal zero because bases differ at each step.
options.trap_reason_hi: symmetry trap — 50−50 = 0 नहीं होता क्योंकि हर step पर base अलग है।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Right magnitude, wrong direction. Successive ±x always nets to a decrease.
options.trap_reason_hi: magnitude सही, दिशा गलत। successive ±x का net हमेशा कमी होता है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Took only the second step. The first step inflated the base.
options.trap_reason_hi: केवल दूसरा step लिया। पहले step ने base बढ़ा दिया था।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Take 100 → +50% → 150 → −50% → 75. Drop = 25 on 100 = 25%. The asymmetry comes from cutting on a larger base (150) than you grew on (100). General rule: ±x% successive on the same number always nets to a decrease of (x²/100)%.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: 100 → +50% → 150 → −50% → 75। drop = 100 में से 25 = 25%। asymmetry का कारण: कमी बड़े base (150) पर लगी, बढ़त छोटे (100) पर। सामान्य नियम: एक ही संख्या पर successive ±x% का net हमेशा (x²/100)% की कमी।

questions.stem_en: An article bought for ₹800 is sold for ₹960. Find the gain percentage.
questions.stem_hi: एक वस्तु ₹800 में खरीदकर ₹960 में बेची गई। gain प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Divided gain 160 by SP 960 (not CP 800): 160/960 = 16.67%. This answers margin% on SP, not gain% on CP.
options.trap_reason_hi: 160 को SP 960 से भाग दिया: 160/960 = 16.67%। यह margin% (SP पर) है, gain% (CP पर) नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted gain 160 from CP first, then used 640 as the denominator: 160/(800−160) = 160/640 = 25%. The denominator must stay CP 800.
options.trap_reason_hi: CP 800 में से gain 160 घटाकर denominator 640 लिया: 160/640 = 25%। Denominator हमेशा CP 800 ही रहेगा।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed SP/CP × 100 = 960/800 × 100 = 120%. This is the selling-price multiplier expressed as a percent (= 1 + gain%), not the gain% itself.
options.trap_reason_hi: SP/CP × 100 = 960/800 × 100 = 120% लिया। यह SP multiplier % में है (1 + gain%), gain% नहीं।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Gain percent always uses cost price as the base: gain% = (SP−CP)/CP × 100. Using SP as the denominator answers a different question (margin %, not gain %) — the values differ whenever there is any non-zero profit or loss.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Gain percent हमेशा cost price पर लगता है: gain% = (SP−CP)/CP × 100। SP को denominator बनाना गलत है — वह margin% निकालता है (अलग धारणा)।

questions.stem_en: The cost price of an article is ₹500. The shopkeeper marks it up by 20%. What is the marked price?
questions.stem_hi: एक वस्तु का cost price ₹500 है। दुकानदार उस पर 20% markup लगाता है। marked price क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Added 20 directly instead of 20% of 500; 20% of 500 = 100, giving ₹600, not ₹520.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Doubled the markup rate to 40%: 500 × 1.40 = 700. The question states 20%, not 40%.
options.trap_reason_hi: markup rate को दोगुना करके 40% लगाया: 500 × 1.40 = 700। Question में 20% दिया है, 40% नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed 500 × 0.80 — took a 20% discount direction instead of a 20% markup.
options.trap_reason_hi: 500 × 0.80 लगाया — markup की जगह 20% discount की दिशा में किया।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Markup is measured as a percentage of cost price: a 20% markup adds 20% of CP (₹100) to CP (₹500), yielding MP ₹600. Markup and discount are inverse direction operations — they move the price away from CP and away from MP respectively.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Markup cost price का percent होता है: 20% markup = CP का 20% (₹100) = ₹500 में जुड़कर MP ₹600। Markup CP से बढ़ाता है, discount MP से घटाता है — दिशा विपरीत है।

questions.stem_en: The marked price of a shirt is ₹600. After a 15% discount, what is the selling price?
questions.stem_hi: एक shirt का marked price ₹600 है। 15% discount के बाद selling price क्या होगी?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 15 (the percent number, not 15% of the amount) directly from 600: 600 − 15 = 585.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Applied a 10% discount (600 × 0.90 = 540), not the 15% stated in the question.
options.trap_reason_hi: 600 × 0.90 = 540 लगाया — 10% discount, जबकि question में 15% है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Added 15% instead of subtracting: 600 × 1.15 = 690. Direction flipped — discount subtracts, not adds.
options.trap_reason_hi: Discount को घटाने की बजाय जोड़ा: 600 × 1.15 = 690। Discount हमेशा घटता है, जुड़ता नहीं।

questions.explanation_method_en: SP = MP × (1 − discount%) = 600 × 0.85 = ₹510. Shortcut: 10% of 600 = 60, 5% = 30, total = 90 off.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Discount is a percentage of the marked price (not of CP). SP = MP − discount. When you flip the operation to a multiplier (0.85 here), the arithmetic collapses to one step — handy in exam conditions.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Discount marked price का percent है (CP का नहीं)। SP = MP − discount। multiplier (यहाँ 0.85) में बदलने से एक step में हल हो जाता है।

questions.stem_en: An article bought for ₹900 is sold for ₹810. Find the loss percentage.
questions.stem_hi: एक वस्तु ₹900 में खरीदकर ₹810 में बेची गई। loss प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Divided loss 90 by SP 810 (not CP 900): 90/810 = 11.11%. Loss% always uses CP as the base.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Substituted denominator 1000 for CP 900 (mental shortcut): 90/1000 = 9%. Correct CP is 900, not 1000.

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed SP/CP × 100 = 810/900 × 100 = 90%. This is SP as a percent of CP (= 100 − loss%), not the loss% itself.
options.trap_reason_hi: SP/CP × 100 = 810/900 × 100 = 90% लगाया। यह CP के percent में SP है (= 100 − loss%), loss% नहीं।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Loss% uses CP as the base just like gain%. Sign is captured by the fact that SP < CP; the formula (CP−SP)/CP × 100 returns a positive percentage representing the shortfall relative to cost.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Loss% में भी base CP होता है (gain% की तरह)। Formula (CP−SP)/CP × 100; positive number shortfall दर्शाता है।

questions.stem_en: A toy is sold for ₹660 at a gain of 10%. What was the cost price?
questions.stem_hi: एक खिलौना ₹660 में 10% gain पर बेचा गया। cost price क्या थी?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 10% of SP from SP: 660 − 66 = 594. CP formula is SP/(1 + gain%), not SP − gain%·SP.
options.trap_reason_hi: SP में से 10% of SP घटाया: 660 − 66 = 594। CP formula SP/(1 + gain%) है।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Added 10% of SP to SP: 660 × 1.10 = 726. Treated SP as the base of gain in the wrong direction.
options.trap_reason_hi: SP में 10% of SP जोड़ दिया: 660 × 1.10 = 726। Gain base को गलत दिशा में लगाया।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Applied reverse formula with 20% in place of 10%: 660/1.20 = 550. Read the gain rate wrong.
options.trap_reason_hi: Reverse formula में 10% की जगह 20% लगाया: 660/1.20 = 550। Gain rate गलत पढ़ा।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Reverse-percentage: given a post-gain SP, the CP is recovered by dividing by the multiplier (1 + gain%). Subtracting the gain% from SP is a common error — it uses SP as the base of the gain, but gain is measured on CP.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Reverse-percentage: gain-post SP से CP निकालने के लिए multiplier (1 + gain%) से भाग दें। SP में से gain% घटाना गलत है — gain का base CP है, SP नहीं।

questions.stem_en: A trader bought a machine for ₹1,200 and sold it at a 15% loss. Find the selling price.
questions.stem_hi: एक व्यापारी ने ₹1,200 में एक machine खरीदी और 15% loss पर बेची। selling price क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 15 (not 15% = ₹180) from CP.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Added the loss instead of subtracting — wrong direction.
options.trap_reason_hi: Loss जोड़ दिया, घटाया नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Used 10% loss (multiplied by 0.90), not 15%.
options.trap_reason_hi: 10% loss लगाया, 15% नहीं।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Loss shifts SP below CP. Multiplying CP by (1 − loss%) gives SP in one step, analogous to how (1 + gain%) handles the gain case. Subtracting the percent value directly (15) from the amount (1200) conflates 'percent' with 'amount'.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Loss में SP, CP से कम हो जाता है। CP × (1 − loss%) एक step में SP देता है। percent value को सीधे amount से घटाना ग़लत है।

questions.stem_en: The marked price of a bag is ₹1,000 and it is sold for ₹850. Find the discount percentage.
questions.stem_hi: एक bag का marked price ₹1,000 है और यह ₹850 में बिकता है। discount प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Divided discount 150 by SP 850 (not MP 1000): 150/850 = 17.65%. Discount% must use MP as the base.
options.trap_reason_hi: Discount 150 को SP 850 से भाग दिया: 150/850 = 17.65%। Discount% का base MP होता है।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used MP + discount = 1150 as denominator: 150/1150 = 13.04%. The denominator is just MP, not MP + discount.
options.trap_reason_hi: Denominator में MP + discount = 1150 लिया: 150/1150 = 13.04%। Denominator सिर्फ MP होता है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Computed SP/MP × 100 = 850/1000 × 100 = 85%. This is the fraction paid (= 100 − discount%), not the discount%.
options.trap_reason_hi: SP/MP × 100 = 85% लगाया — यह pay किया गया अंश है (= 100 − discount%), discount% नहीं।

questions.explanation_method_en: Discount = MP − SP = 150. Discount% = 150/1000 × 100 = 15%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Discount% is always expressed relative to the marked price, not the selling price. Using SP as the denominator inflates the number because SP < MP, so dividing by the smaller denominator gives a larger apparent rate.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Discount = MP − SP = 150। Discount% = 150/1000 × 100 = 15%।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Discount% हमेशा marked price पर होता है, SP पर नहीं। SP से भाग देने पर denominator छोटा होता है (SP<MP) अतः answer बड़ा आता है — गलत।

questions.stem_en: Which option represents a larger absolute profit: selling at 20% gain on a CP of ₹200, or selling at 15% gain on a CP of ₹300?
questions.stem_hi: किसमें absolute profit ज़्यादा है: CP ₹200 पर 20% gain, या CP ₹300 पर 15% gain?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: 20% of 200 = 40, which is less than 15% of 300 = 45.

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Incorrect assumption; do the arithmetic: 40 ≠ 45.
options.trap_reason_hi: गलत मान्यता; 40 ≠ 45।

options.label: D
options.text_hi: जानकारी कम है, compare नहीं कर सकते
options.trap_reason_en: Both percentages are tied to specific amounts — a definite comparison is possible.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों percentages specific amounts से जुड़े हैं — comparison हो सकता है।

questions.explanation_concept_en: Percentages alone don't compare absolute profits — the base matters. A smaller percent on a larger base can produce more absolute profit. Always multiply the percent by the amount before comparing.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Percentage अकेले absolute profit compare नहीं करता — base मायने रखता है। बड़े base पर छोटा percent बड़ा profit दे सकता है।

questions.stem_en: A watch is marked at ₹2,000. The shopkeeper offers a 10% discount, then a further 5% on the reduced price. What is the final selling price?
questions.stem_hi: एक watch का marked price ₹2,000 है। दुकानदार पहले 10% discount देता है, फिर reduced price पर 5% और। अंतिम selling price क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted 15% directly from 2000 — successive discounts compound, they don't add.
options.trap_reason_hi: 2000 से 15% सीधे घटाया — successive discounts compound होते हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Only applied the first discount (10% → 1800); missed the second 5%.
options.trap_reason_hi: सिर्फ पहला discount (10% → 1800) लगाया; दूसरा 5% छूट गया।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Applied 5% on the original 2000 (not on 1800) for the second step.

questions.explanation_concept_en: Successive discounts multiply: final factor = (1 − d₁/100)(1 − d₂/100). The equivalent single rate is always smaller than the sum (here 14.5% < 15%) because the second discount applies to an already-shrunk base.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Successive discounts multiply होते हैं: factor = (1 − d₁/100)(1 − d₂/100)। Equivalent single हमेशा sum से कम (यहाँ 14.5% < 15%)।

questions.stem_en: A store offers two successive discounts of 20% and 10%. What is the equivalent single discount percentage?
questions.stem_hi: एक store दो successive discounts देता है: 20% और 10%। equivalent single discount प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Added 20 + 10 — successive percentages compound, they don't sum.
options.trap_reason_hi: 20+10 जोड़ दिया — successive percentages compound होते हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Close but computed 20 + 10 − (20×10)/200 with wrong divisor; correct formula uses /100.
options.trap_reason_hi: formula में divisor 200 लगाया; सही /100 है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Overshot: multiplied factors 0.80 × 0.88 mistakenly (wrong second factor).
options.trap_reason_hi: second factor गलत ले लिया।

questions.explanation_concept_en: For two successive discounts a% and b% the overall multiplier is (1 − a/100)(1 − b/100). Expanding gives 1 − a/100 − b/100 + ab/10000. The equivalent single discount is a + b − ab/100 (in percent). The ab/100 'correction' term explains why successive discounts always beat the user (the combined rate is less than the sum).
questions.explanation_concept_hi: दो successive discounts a%, b% का overall multiplier (1−a/100)(1−b/100) होता है। equivalent single = a + b − ab/100। यह correction term बताता है कि successive discount हमेशा sum से कम होता है।

questions.stem_en: Two articles are sold for ₹960 each. On one the trader gains 20%, on the other he loses 20%. What is his overall profit or loss percentage?
questions.stem_hi: दो articles ₹960-₹960 में बिकते हैं। एक पर 20% gain, दूसरे पर 20% loss हुआ। overall profit/loss प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Symmetric cancellation — +20% and −20% at the same SP do NOT cancel because the underlying CPs (800 and 1200) differ.
options.trap_reason_hi: +20% और −20% same SP पर cancel नहीं होते क्योंकि CPs (800 और 1200) अलग हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Correct magnitude but wrong sign — with same SP and equal-% split, the heavier CP (loss side) dominates, so the net is always a LOSS.
options.trap_reason_hi: Magnitude सही, sign गलत — same SP और equal % split में बड़ी CP (loss side) dominate करती है, net हमेशा LOSS।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Applied only the −20% loss side and ignored the +20% gain side entirely: reported the loss% as the overall P/L%.
options.trap_reason_hi: सिर्फ −20% loss side लगाया, +20% gain side पूरी तरह छोड़ दिया: overall P/L% को loss% के बराबर मान लिया।

questions.explanation_method_en: Shortcut: whenever SPs are equal and the two percentages are the same x%, overall loss = (x/10)² = (20/10)² = 4%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: CP of the 20%-gain article = 960/1.20 = 800. CP of the 20%-loss article = 960/0.80 = 1200. Total CP = 2000, total SP = 1920. Loss = 80 on 2000 = 4%. The asymmetry comes from the CPs differing: the loss article's CP is higher (so the percent loss is on a bigger base) than the gain article's.
questions.explanation_method_hi: shortcut: जब दोनों SP बराबर और percentages भी (x%), तो net loss = (x/10)² = (20/10)² = 4%।

questions.stem_en: An article is marked up by 40% above its cost and then sold after a 20% discount. Find the gain percentage.
questions.stem_hi: एक वस्तु पर 40% markup लगाकर फिर 20% discount पर बेची गई। gain प्रतिशत क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted directly: m − d = 40 − 20 = 20%. Ignored that markup uses CP as base (100) while discount uses MP as base (140).
options.trap_reason_hi: सीधे m − d = 40 − 20 = 20% निकाला। Markup CP (100) पर, discount MP (140) पर — bases अलग।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Sign error on the cross-term: computed 40 − 20 + (40×20)/100 = 40 − 20 + 8 = 28. The correct formula is m − d − md/100 (minus sign on cross-term).
options.trap_reason_hi: Cross-term का sign गलत: 40 − 20 + (40×20)/100 = 28 निकाला। सही formula m − d − md/100 है (− sign on cross-term)।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Halved the naive difference (40 − 20 = 20) as a 'compounding dampener': got 10%. True dampening = only the cross-term md/100 = 4, giving 16... actually 20 − 8 = 12%, not 10.
options.trap_reason_hi: Naive अंतर (40 − 20 = 20) को आधा कर दिया ('compounding dampener' सोचकर): 10% मिला। सही dampening केवल cross-term md/100 = 8 है।

questions.explanation_concept_en: The markup inflates CP to MP; the discount deflates MP to SP. Net gain depends on whether (1 + markup)(1 − discount) is greater or less than 1. Here it's 1.12, so 12% gain. Adding markup% and subtracting discount% is always wrong unless you explicitly use the compound-percentage formula a + b + (ab/100).
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Markup CP से MP बढ़ाता है; discount MP से SP घटाता है। Net gain (1+markup)(1−discount) पर निर्भर — यहाँ 1.12 → 12% gain। Markup से discount सीधे घटाना गलत है जब तक compound formula न लगाएँ।

questions.stem_en: A shopkeeper sells goods at cost price but uses a weight of 900g in place of 1 kg. What is his actual gain percentage?
questions.stem_hi: एक दुकानदार cost price पर सामान बेचता है पर 1 kg की जगह 900g का बाट (weight) इस्तेमाल करता है। वास्तविक gain% क्या है?

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Used shortfall 100 divided by claimed weight 1000: 100/1000 = 10%. The denominator must be what was actually delivered (900g), not claimed.
options.trap_reason_hi: Shortfall 100 को claimed weight 1000 से भाग दिया: 100/1000 = 10%। Denominator delivered weight (900g) होगा।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Divided shortfall 100 by 1100 (added shortfall to claimed weight by mistake): 100/1100 = 9.09%. Denominator is 900, not 1100.
options.trap_reason_hi: Shortfall 100 को 1100 से भाग दिया (shortfall को claimed weight में जोड़ दिया): 100/1100 = 9.09%। Denominator 900 है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Assumed shortfall was 100 on a base of 800 (interpreted 'delivered 800g while claiming 1000g'): 100/800 = 12.5%. The actual delivered weight is 900g.
options.trap_reason_hi: 800g को delivered weight मान लिया (shortfall 200g माना): 100/800 = 12.5%। सही delivered weight 900g है।

questions.explanation_method_en: Shortcut: gain% = (error / (true wt − error)) × 100 = (100/900) × 100 = 11.11%.
questions.explanation_concept_en: The merchant's cost per true-gram stays the same, but he only delivers 900g while charging the price of 1000g. So he saves the cost of 100g for every 900g he actually gives. Gain percent is therefore 100/900 (the extra revenue divided by what was actually spent), not 100/1000 (which would be the fraction of the claimed weight).
questions.explanation_method_hi: shortcut: gain% = (error / (true wt − error)) × 100 = (100/900) × 100 = 11.11%।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Merchant true-gram की cost वही रखता है पर केवल 900g देता है और 1000g की कीमत वसूलता है। हर 900g पर 100g की cost बचाता है। Gain% = 100/900, न कि 100/1000।

questions.stem_en: 10 kg of rice at ₹10 per kg is mixed with 10 kg at ₹20 per kg. The mixture is sold at ₹18 per kg. Find the gain percentage on the mixture.
questions.stem_hi: 10 kg चावल ₹10 per kg वाला और 10 kg ₹20 per kg वाला मिलाकर mixture ₹18 per kg पर बेचा गया। mixture पर gain% क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Divided gain 60 by cheap-variety CP only (10 × 10 = 100): 60/100 = 60%. Denominator must be total mix CP = 300.
options.trap_reason_hi: Gain 60 को सिर्फ cheap variety की CP (10 × 10 = 100) से भाग दिया: 60/100 = 60%। सही total mix CP 300।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Assumed total CP at the higher rate: 20 kg × ₹20 = ₹400 → 60/400 = 15%. Actual mix CP = 10×10 + 10×20 = 300.

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Divided gain 60 by total SP 360 (not CP 300): 60/360 = 16.67%. Gain% is always on CP.
options.trap_reason_hi: Gain 60 को total SP 360 से भाग दिया: 60/360 = 16.67%। Gain% हमेशा CP पर।

questions.explanation_concept_en: When two costs are mixed and sold at a single rate, total CP is the sum of the two inputs and total SP is (final per-unit rate × total units). Gain% is always on total CP, not on either input's CP alone.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: दो costs mix होकर एक rate पर बिकते हैं: total CP = sum of inputs, total SP = per-unit rate × total units। Gain% कुल CP पर।

questions.stem_en: A trader buys a product for ₹500. He spends ₹50 on transport and sells it for ₹660. Find his gain percentage.
questions.stem_hi: एक व्यापारी ₹500 में product खरीदता है, ₹50 transport पर खर्च करता है, और ₹660 में बेचता है। gain% क्या है?

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Ignored transport when computing CP: used gain 160 (= 660 − 500) over CP 500: 160/500 = 32%. Correct total cost = 500 + 50 = 550, gain = 110.
options.trap_reason_hi: Transport छोड़ दिया: gain 160 (= 660 − 500) को CP 500 पर: 160/500 = 32%। सही total cost = 500 + 50 = 550, gain = 110।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Used correct gain 110 but ignored transport only in the denominator: 110/500 = 22%. Transport 50 must be included in CP, so 110/550 = 20%.
options.trap_reason_hi: Gain 110 सही, पर denominator में transport छूट गया: 110/500 = 22%। Transport 50 CP में जोड़ना है: 110/550 = 20%।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Subtracted transport from CP instead of adding: 500 − 50 = 450; 110/450 = 24.4%. Transport is a cost — it ADDS to CP, not subtracts.
options.trap_reason_hi: Transport को CP में से घटा दिया: 500 − 50 = 450; 110/450 = 24.4%। Transport cost है, यह CP में जुड़ती है।

questions.explanation_concept_en: All overheads (transport, storage, packaging) are part of the cost price. Ignoring them inflates the apparent gain%. Always aggregate every pre-sale expense into CP before computing gain%.
questions.explanation_concept_hi: सारे overheads (transport, storage, packaging) CP में जुड़ते हैं। इन्हें भूलने पर apparent gain% बढ़ जाता है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All flowers are red. All red things are beautiful. Conclusions: I. All flowers are beautiful. II. Some beautiful things are flowers.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी flowers red हैं। सभी red चीज़ें beautiful हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. सभी flowers beautiful हैं। II. कुछ beautiful चीज़ें flowers हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: Conclusion II also follows: if all flowers are beautiful (and flowers exist), then at least some beautiful things are flowers — by particular-from-universal extraction.
options.trap_reason_hi: II भी सही है: यदि सभी flowers beautiful हैं (और flowers मौजूद हैं), तो कुछ beautiful चीज़ें flowers होंगी।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: Conclusion I also follows: All flowers ⊆ All red ⊆ All beautiful gives All flowers ⊆ All beautiful directly (transitive chain of universals).
options.trap_reason_hi: I भी valid है: सभी flowers ⊆ सभी red ⊆ सभी beautiful — universal transitive से All flowers ⊆ All beautiful।

options.label: C
options.text_hi: I और II दोनों अनुसरण करते हैं

options.label: D
options.text_hi: न I न II अनुसरण करता है
options.trap_reason_en: Both conclusions follow logically — flatly rejecting both ignores the valid universal-transitive chain.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों conclusions valid हैं — दोनों को reject करना universal-transitive chain को ignore करना है।

questions.explanation_method_en: All flowers ⊆ red, all red ⊆ beautiful ⇒ all flowers ⊆ beautiful (transitive). I follows. From 'all flowers are beautiful' and the existence of flowers, 'some beautiful are flowers' follows (existential extraction). II follows. Hence both follow.
questions.explanation_concept_en: A universal affirmative chain (All A ⊆ B, all B ⊆ C) gives All A ⊆ C. From any universal 'all A are X' with non-empty A, the particular 'some X are A' is valid — this is called the existential import of universal statements.
questions.explanation_method_hi: All flowers ⊆ red, all red ⊆ beautiful ⇒ all flowers ⊆ beautiful (transitive)। I valid है। 'सभी flowers beautiful हैं' से 'कुछ beautiful चीज़ें flowers हैं' valid है (existential import)। II भी valid। दोनों follow करते हैं।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Universal affirmative chain (All A ⊆ B, all B ⊆ C) से All A ⊆ C। 'All A are X' (A non-empty) से 'Some X are A' valid (existential import)।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All cats are mammals. No mammal is a fish. Conclusions: I. No cat is a fish. II. Some mammals are not cats.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी cats mammals हैं। कोई mammal fish नहीं है। निष्कर्ष: I. कोई cat fish नहीं है। II. कुछ mammals cats नहीं हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is NOT necessarily true: if all mammals were cats (consistent with the premises), then no mammal would be a non-cat. The premises don't rule that out.
options.trap_reason_hi: II ज़रूरी नहीं: यदि सभी mammals cats हों (premises के साथ consistent), तो कोई mammal non-cat नहीं होगा।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows but II does not. Don't infer 'some mammals are not cats' from a one-way subset; mammals could equal cats.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid पर II invalid। एक-तरफ़ा subset से 'कुछ mammals cats नहीं' नहीं निकलता।

options.label: D
options.text_hi: न I न II अनुसरण करता है
options.trap_reason_en: I follows: cats ⊆ mammals and mammals ∩ fish = ∅ ⇒ cats ∩ fish = ∅. Rejecting both ignores this valid chain.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid है: cats ⊆ mammals, mammals ∩ fish = ∅ ⇒ cats ∩ fish = ∅।

questions.explanation_method_en: Cats ⊆ mammals; mammals ∩ fish = ∅. Therefore cats ∩ fish = ∅. So I follows. II would require knowing that mammals strictly contains cats, but the premises allow mammals = cats.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Subset relations chain through disjoint relations: A ⊆ B and B ∩ C = ∅ ⇒ A ∩ C = ∅. But A ⊆ B does NOT imply 'some B are not A' unless we additionally know A ⊊ B (proper subset). The premises here allow equality.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Cats ⊆ mammals; mammals ∩ fish = ∅ ⇒ cats ∩ fish = ∅। I valid। II के लिए mammals ⊋ cats चाहिए, पर premises equality allow करते हैं।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Subset और disjoint chain होते हैं: A ⊆ B और B ∩ C = ∅ ⇒ A ∩ C = ∅। पर A ⊆ B से 'कुछ B, A नहीं हैं' नहीं निकलता (proper subset चाहिए)।

questions.stem_en: Statements: Some pens are pencils. All pencils are erasers. Conclusions: I. Some pens are erasers. II. All erasers are pencils.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कुछ pens, pencils हैं। सभी pencils, erasers हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ pens, erasers हैं। II. सभी erasers, pencils हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is improper conversion: 'All pencils are erasers' does NOT mean 'All erasers are pencils'. Erasers could include non-pencils.
options.trap_reason_hi: II improper conversion है: 'सभी pencils erasers हैं' का मतलब 'सभी erasers pencils' नहीं — erasers में non-pencils भी हो सकते हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows from chaining 'some pens are pencils' with 'all pencils are erasers'. II is invalid (improper conversion of universal affirmative).
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid है chain से। II improper conversion से invalid।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: I does follow: those pens that are pencils (existing by 'some pens are pencils') must also be erasers (by 'all pencils are erasers'), so 'some pens are erasers' holds.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid है: 'कुछ pens जो pencils हैं' सभी erasers होंगे, अतः 'कुछ pens erasers हैं' सच है।

questions.explanation_method_en: Some pens are pencils ⇒ at least one item is both pen and pencil. All pencils are erasers ⇒ that item is also an eraser ⇒ some pens are erasers. I follows. II is improper conversion of a universal affirmative — invalid.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Particular affirmative + universal affirmative gives particular affirmative on the other side: 'Some A are B' + 'All B are C' ⇒ 'Some A are C'. The converse of 'All B are C' is NOT 'All C are B' — universal affirmative does not validly convert.
questions.explanation_method_hi: 'कुछ pens pencils हैं' से कम-से-कम एक चीज़ pen-और-pencil दोनों है। 'सभी pencils erasers हैं' से वह eraser भी है ⇒ 'कुछ pens erasers हैं'। I valid। II universal affirmative का improper conversion।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Particular affirmative + universal affirmative ⇒ particular affirmative: 'Some A are B' + 'All B are C' ⇒ 'Some A are C'। 'All B are C' का converse 'All C are B' valid नहीं।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All teachers are educated. Some educated people are doctors. Conclusions: I. Some teachers are doctors. II. Some doctors are teachers.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी teachers educated हैं। कुछ educated लोग doctors हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ teachers doctors हैं। II. कुछ doctors teachers हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I does NOT follow: the 'some educated people who are doctors' might be entirely outside the teacher subset. Undistributed middle.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid नहीं: 'कुछ educated जो doctors हैं' teacher subset के बाहर हो सकते हैं। Undistributed middle।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is the converse of I and equally invalid. The middle term 'educated' is not distributed in either premise.
options.trap_reason_hi: II, I का converse और उतना ही invalid है। Middle term 'educated' किसी भी premise में distributed नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Both fail by the same fallacy: the doctors mentioned could be educated non-teachers; teachers and doctors might not overlap at all.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों एक ही fallacy से fail: doctors educated-non-teachers हो सकते हैं — teachers और doctors में overlap ज़रूरी नहीं।

questions.explanation_method_en: Teachers ⊆ educated. Some educated are doctors — but the 'some' might lie entirely outside teachers. So we cannot conclude that any teacher is a doctor or vice versa. Neither follows.
questions.explanation_concept_en: The classical fallacy of undistributed middle: when a particular statement involves the middle term, that middle term may not link the two extremes. Both 'all A are B' and 'some B are C' share B, but the 'some B' could miss A entirely.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Teachers ⊆ educated। 'कुछ educated, doctors हैं' — पर ये 'कुछ' teachers के बाहर हो सकते हैं। न I न II।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Undistributed middle का classical fallacy: middle term particular में हो तो वह दोनों extremes को link नहीं करता।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All birds can fly. Penguins are birds. Conclusions: I. Penguins can fly. II. Some flying things are penguins.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी birds उड़ सकते हैं। Penguins birds हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. Penguins उड़ सकते हैं। II. कुछ उड़ने वाली चीज़ें penguins हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: II also follows: from 'penguins fly' + 'penguins exist', 'some flying things are penguins' is a valid existential extraction.
options.trap_reason_hi: II भी valid: 'penguins उड़ते हैं' + 'penguins मौजूद हैं' से 'कुछ उड़ने वाले penguins हैं' निकलता है।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: I follows by direct modus ponens: All birds fly, penguins are birds ⇒ penguins fly. Real-world knowledge that penguins don't fly is irrelevant; treat premises as true.
options.trap_reason_hi: I direct modus ponens से valid: सभी birds उड़ते हैं + penguins birds हैं ⇒ penguins उड़ते हैं। Real-world ज्ञान irrelevant; premises को सच मानें।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Both follow under the syllogism rule: treat the given premises as true regardless of real-world facts.
options.trap_reason_hi: Syllogism rule से दोनों valid हैं: premises को real-world से अलग सच मानें।

questions.explanation_method_en: Treat premises as true. All birds ⊆ flying things; penguins ⊆ birds ⇒ penguins ⊆ flying things ⇒ I follows. From 'penguins fly' and 'penguins exist', 'some flying things are penguins' follows ⇒ II follows.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Syllogism asks you to treat the given premises as true, even if they conflict with real-world knowledge. The instruction 'take the statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts' is the syllogism cardinal rule.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Premises को सच मानें। All birds ⊆ flying; penguins ⊆ birds ⇒ penguins ⊆ flying ⇒ I valid। 'Penguins उड़ते हैं' + 'penguins मौजूद हैं' से II valid।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Syllogism में real-world से अलग premises को सच मानना है। यह syllogism का cardinal rule है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: No book is a magazine. Some magazines are journals. Conclusions: I. Some journals are not books. II. Some books are not journals.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कोई book magazine नहीं है। कुछ magazines journals हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ journals books नहीं हैं। II. कुछ books journals नहीं हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is invalid: the premises say nothing about whether all books are or aren't journals. Books could be journals (just not magazines).
options.trap_reason_hi: II invalid: premises books और journals के बीच कोई direct constraint नहीं देते। Books, journals हो सकती हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows but II does not. The premises do not constrain books-journals overlap directly.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid पर II invalid। Premises books-journals direct overlap पर कुछ नहीं कहते।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: I does follow: 'some magazines are journals' identifies items that are magazines AND journals; those items can't be books (since no book is a magazine), so 'some journals are not books'.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid है: 'कुछ magazines journals हैं' से magazine-AND-journal items हैं; ये books नहीं हो सकते (books, magazines नहीं), अतः 'कुछ journals books नहीं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: Some magazines are journals ⇒ ∃x: x is magazine AND x is journal. 'No book is magazine' ⇒ that x is not a book ⇒ that x is journal-and-not-book ⇒ some journals are not books. I follows. II talks about books and journals directly; nothing in premises forces a non-overlap there.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Combining a particular affirmative ('some B are J') with a universal negative ('no Bk is B') yields a particular negative ('some J are not Bk'). The other direction ('some Bk are not J') is not forced.
questions.explanation_method_hi: 'कुछ magazines journals हैं' ⇒ कोई item magazine AND journal है। 'कोई book magazine नहीं' ⇒ वह item book नहीं ⇒ कुछ journals books नहीं। I valid। II के लिए premises books-journals पर कुछ नहीं कहते।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Particular affirmative + universal negative ⇒ particular negative। Reverse direction (II) force नहीं होता।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All apples are red. All red are round. All round are sweet. Conclusions: I. All apples are sweet. II. Some sweet are apples.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी apples red हैं। सभी red round हैं। सभी round sweet हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. सभी apples sweet हैं। II. कुछ sweet, apples हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: II also follows: from 'all apples are sweet' and existence of apples, 'some sweet are apples' is a valid existential extraction.
options.trap_reason_hi: II भी valid: 'सभी apples sweet' से (apples मौजूद मानकर) 'कुछ sweet apples हैं' निकलता है।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: I follows from a three-step transitive chain. Rejecting I is rejecting transitivity of the universal affirmative.
options.trap_reason_hi: I three-step transitive से valid है। I को reject करना universal affirmative की transitivity reject करना है।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: Both follow logically; rejecting both ignores the universal-transitive chain and existential import.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों valid; reject करना universal transitive और existential import दोनों ignore करना है।

questions.explanation_method_en: Apples ⊆ red ⊆ round ⊆ sweet (transitive). I: all apples are sweet ✓. II: with apples non-empty, some sweet are apples ✓. Both follow.
questions.explanation_concept_en: A chain of universal affirmatives is transitive: A ⊆ B ⊆ C ⊆ D ⇒ A ⊆ D. Existential extraction from any 'all A are X' (with A non-empty) yields 'some X are A'.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Apples ⊆ red ⊆ round ⊆ sweet (transitive)। I valid; II existential extraction से valid (apples non-empty)।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Universal affirmative chain transitive: A ⊆ B ⊆ C ⊆ D ⇒ A ⊆ D। Non-empty A से 'all A are X' पर 'some X are A' निकलता है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: Some students are clever. All clever people are successful. Conclusions: I. Some students are successful. II. All successful people are students.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कुछ students clever हैं। सभी clever लोग successful हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ students successful हैं। II. सभी successful लोग students हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is improper conversion of 'All clever people are successful' via the chained 'students are successful' step. Universal affirmatives don't convert validly.
options.trap_reason_hi: II improper conversion है। Universal affirmative convert valid नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows but II is improper conversion. Successful people might include non-students; nothing in premises rules that out.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid पर II improper conversion। Successful में non-students हो सकते हैं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: I does follow: those students who are clever (existing by 'some students are clever') are also successful (by 'all clever are successful').
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid: 'कुछ students जो clever हैं' सभी successful हैं ⇒ 'कुछ students successful हैं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: Some students are clever ⇒ ∃ student who is clever. All clever are successful ⇒ that student is successful ⇒ some students are successful. I follows. II would require successful ⊆ students, but premises only give clever ⊆ successful — successful could include non-clever non-students.
questions.explanation_concept_en: 'Some A are B' + 'All B are C' ⇒ 'Some A are C'. Converting 'all B are C' to 'all C are B' is improper — the standard fallacy of illicit conversion of universal affirmative.
questions.explanation_method_hi: 'कुछ students clever' से कोई student clever है। 'All clever successful' से वह student successful ⇒ 'कुछ students successful'। II के लिए successful ⊆ students चाहिए — premises नहीं देते।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Some A are B + All B are C ⇒ Some A are C। 'All B are C' को 'All C are B' में convert करना improper है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All squares are rectangles. All rectangles are quadrilaterals. Conclusions: I. All quadrilaterals are squares. II. Some quadrilaterals are squares.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी squares rectangles हैं। सभी rectangles quadrilaterals हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. सभी quadrilaterals squares हैं। II. कुछ quadrilaterals squares हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is improper conversion of universal affirmative — squares ⊆ quadrilaterals does NOT mean quadrilaterals ⊆ squares.
options.trap_reason_hi: I improper conversion: squares ⊆ quadrilaterals का मतलब quadrilaterals ⊆ squares नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: II follows by existential import; I is improper conversion. Don't accept 'all C are A' from 'all A are C'.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid पर I improper conversion।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: II follows: with squares non-empty (subset of rectangles, etc.), 'some quadrilaterals are squares' is a valid existential extraction.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid: squares non-empty ⇒ 'कुछ quadrilaterals squares हैं' existential import से।

questions.explanation_method_en: Squares ⊆ rectangles ⊆ quadrilaterals. So all squares are quadrilaterals. With squares non-empty, 'some quadrilaterals are squares' (existential extraction). II follows. I requires the converse, which is invalid.
questions.explanation_concept_en: From any universal affirmative 'all A are B' with A non-empty, the particular 'some B are A' follows by existential import. The full converse 'all B are A' does NOT.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Squares ⊆ rectangles ⊆ quadrilaterals ⇒ सभी squares quadrilaterals हैं। Non-empty squares से 'कुछ quadrilaterals squares' (existential import)। II valid। I converse — invalid।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Non-empty A वाले 'all A are B' से 'some B are A' (existential import से) निकलता है, पर 'all B are A' नहीं।

questions.stem_en: Statements: Some bottles are jars. No jar is a bucket. Conclusions: I. No bottle is a bucket. II. Some bottles are not buckets.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कुछ bottles jars हैं। कोई jar bucket नहीं है। निष्कर्ष: I. कोई bottle bucket नहीं है। II. कुछ bottles, buckets नहीं हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I claims NO bottle is a bucket — too strong. Only the bottles-that-are-jars are forced to be non-buckets; other bottles could be buckets.
options.trap_reason_hi: I बहुत strong: केवल वे bottles जो jars हैं non-buckets हैं; बाकी bottles, buckets हो सकती हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: II follows but I overstates. The premises only constrain bottles-that-are-jars to be non-buckets, not all bottles.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid पर I overstates। Premises सिर्फ bottle-AND-jar items को non-buckets बनाती हैं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: II follows: those bottles that are jars (existing) cannot be buckets; therefore 'some bottles are not buckets'.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid: bottle-जो-jar हैं वे buckets नहीं हो सकते ⇒ 'कुछ bottles buckets नहीं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: Some bottles are jars ⇒ ∃ x: bottle AND jar. No jar is bucket ⇒ x is not a bucket ⇒ some bottles are not buckets. II follows. I claims NO bottle is a bucket, but the premises only constrain bottle-AND-jar items.
questions.explanation_concept_en: From 'some A are B' and 'no B is C', we get 'some A are not C' — but NOT 'no A is C'. The universal negative requires all A to be excluded from C; we only know the AB-overlap is excluded.
questions.explanation_method_hi: 'कुछ bottles, jars' से कोई item bottle AND jar। 'कोई jar, bucket नहीं' ⇒ वह bucket नहीं ⇒ 'कुछ bottles buckets नहीं'। II valid। I के लिए सब bottles excluded होने चाहिए — premises नहीं देते।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Some A are B + No B is C ⇒ Some A are not C। पर No A is C नहीं — सिर्फ AB-overlap excluded है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. Some C are not A.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी A, B हैं। कुछ B, C हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ A, C हैं। II. कुछ C, A नहीं हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is undistributed middle: the 'some B that are C' might lie outside the A subset entirely.
options.trap_reason_hi: I undistributed middle: 'कुछ B जो C हैं' A के बाहर हो सकते हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is also not forced: 'some C that are B' might all be A as well — premises allow that.
options.trap_reason_hi: II force नहीं: 'कुछ C जो B हैं' सब A भी हो सकते हैं — premises allow करती हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Both fail: classic undistributed-middle case where the particular term (C) is independent of A in the premises.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों fail: classic undistributed-middle case।

questions.explanation_method_en: All A ⊆ B. Some B are C — but those C's might be in A or outside it; both consistent with premises. Therefore neither 'some A are C' nor 'some C are not A' is forced.
questions.explanation_concept_en: When the middle term B is universal in one premise and particular in the other, but the 'some B' could be any sub-portion of B (including or excluding A), no conclusion connecting A and C is forced. This is the canonical undistributed-middle setup.
questions.explanation_method_hi: All A ⊆ B। 'कुछ B, C' — पर वे A के अंदर या बाहर हो सकते हैं; दोनों premises के साथ consistent। न 'कुछ A, C' न 'कुछ C, A नहीं' force होते हैं।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Middle term एक premise में universal, दूसरे में particular हो, पर 'some B' any sub-portion (A में या बाहर) हो सकता है, तो A-C connection force नहीं — canonical undistributed-middle।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All cups are plates. All plates are dishes. Conclusions: I. Some dishes are cups. II. All dishes are cups.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी cups plates हैं। सभी plates dishes हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ dishes cups हैं। II. सभी dishes cups हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is improper conversion of the universal affirmative chain. The chain only gives cups ⊆ dishes, NOT dishes ⊆ cups.
options.trap_reason_hi: II improper conversion: chain से cups ⊆ dishes मिलता है, dishes ⊆ cups नहीं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows by existential import; II is the invalid converse. Don't claim 'all C are A' from 'all A are C'.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid (existential import); II improper conversion।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: I follows: cups non-empty ⇒ some dishes are cups (existential extraction).
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid: cups non-empty ⇒ 'कुछ dishes cups' existential import से।

questions.explanation_method_en: Cups ⊆ plates ⊆ dishes. With cups non-empty, some dishes are cups ⇒ I follows. II requires dishes ⊆ cups, which is the invalid converse.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Existential import gives 'some C are A' from 'all A are C' (A non-empty) — that's I. The full converse 'all C are A' is invalid — that's II.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Cups ⊆ plates ⊆ dishes। Non-empty cups से 'कुछ dishes cups' (I valid)। II के लिए dishes ⊆ cups चाहिए — invalid converse।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Non-empty A से 'all A are C' पर 'some C are A' (existential import) — I valid। 'All C are A' invalid converse — II invalid।

questions.stem_en: Statements: No politician is honest. Some honest people are leaders. Conclusions: I. Some leaders are not politicians. II. Some politicians are not leaders.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कोई politician honest नहीं है। कुछ honest लोग leaders हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ leaders politicians नहीं हैं। II. कुछ politicians leaders नहीं हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is not forced: all politicians could simultaneously be leaders (just not honest); premises don't constrain politicians-leaders overlap.
options.trap_reason_hi: II force नहीं: सभी politicians, leaders हो सकते हैं (बस honest नहीं); premises politicians-leaders overlap पर silent।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: I follows from honest+leader items being non-politicians. II is not forced.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid; II force नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: I does follow: those honest people who are leaders cannot be politicians (no politician is honest), so some leaders are not politicians.
options.trap_reason_hi: I valid: honest+leader items politicians नहीं हो सकते (कोई politician honest नहीं), अतः 'कुछ leaders politicians नहीं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: Some honest are leaders ⇒ ∃ x: honest AND leader. No politician is honest ⇒ x is not a politician ⇒ x is leader-and-not-politician ⇒ some leaders are not politicians. I follows. II concerns politicians vs. leaders, but premises don't link them directly.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Particular affirmative + universal negative through a shared term yields a particular negative on the OTHER pair. Direction matters: II asks about politicians-leaders, where the premises give no such link.
questions.explanation_method_hi: 'कुछ honest, leaders' से honest-AND-leader items हैं। 'कोई politician honest नहीं' ⇒ ये items politicians नहीं ⇒ 'कुछ leaders politicians नहीं'। I valid। II politicians-leaders direct link के बिना force नहीं।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Particular affirmative + universal negative shared term से दूसरी pair पर particular negative। Direction मायने रखती है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All P are Q. Some Q are R. No R is S. Conclusions: I. Some P are R. II. Some Q are not S.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी P, Q हैं। कुछ Q, R हैं। कोई R, S नहीं है। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ P, R हैं। II. कुछ Q, S नहीं हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is undistributed middle: the 'some Q that are R' might be entirely outside P even though all P are Q. Specifically, P could be a sub-portion of Q that misses R.
options.trap_reason_hi: I undistributed middle: 'कुछ Q जो R हैं' P के बाहर हो सकते हैं भले all P ⊆ Q।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: II follows but I has the undistributed-middle problem.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid पर I undistributed-middle से fail।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: II follows: 'some Q are R' + 'no R is S' ⇒ those Q-AND-R items are not S ⇒ some Q are not S.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid: 'कुछ Q, R' + 'कोई R, S नहीं' ⇒ Q-AND-R items, S नहीं ⇒ 'कुछ Q, S नहीं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: I: needs P-R link via Q. P ⊆ Q gives info about P, but 'some Q are R' could be the non-P portion of Q. So I not forced. II: ∃ x: Q AND R; no R is S ⇒ x is not S ⇒ x is Q-and-not-S ⇒ some Q are not S. II follows.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Multi-step syllogisms succeed only when the middle terms are properly distributed. Here R links Q and S validly (II) but Q's particular subset that overlaps R is independent of P, blocking I.
questions.explanation_method_hi: I के लिए P-R link via Q चाहिए, पर 'कुछ Q, R' P के बाहर हो सकते हैं ⇒ undistributed middle। II valid: 'कुछ Q, R' + 'कोई R, S नहीं' ⇒ Q-AND-R items S नहीं ⇒ 'कुछ Q, S नहीं'।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Multi-step syllogism में middle terms properly distributed होने चाहिए। यहाँ R, Q-S को link करता है (II) पर Q-R overlap P से independent (I block)।

questions.stem_en: Statements: Some pencils are pens. Some pens are markers. Conclusions: I. Some pencils are markers. II. Some markers are pencils.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कुछ pencils, pens हैं। कुछ pens, markers हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ pencils, markers हैं। II. कुछ markers, pencils हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I cannot be derived from two particulars. The 'some pens that are pencils' and 'some pens that are markers' might be entirely different subsets of pens.
options.trap_reason_hi: I two particulars से नहीं निकलता: 'कुछ pens जो pencils हैं' और 'कुछ pens जो markers हैं' अलग subsets हो सकते हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II is the converse of I and equally invalid. Two particulars don't produce a connecting particular.
options.trap_reason_hi: II, I का converse, उतना ही invalid। दो particulars से connecting particular नहीं निकलता।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Standard rule: a syllogism with two particular premises produces no valid conclusion (both fail by undistributed middle).
options.trap_reason_hi: Standard rule: दो particular premises से कोई valid conclusion नहीं — दोनों undistributed middle से fail।

questions.explanation_method_en: Two particular premises share term 'pens'. The 'some pens that are pencils' subset and the 'some pens that are markers' subset can be disjoint in pen-space, blocking any pencil-marker conclusion.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Classical syllogism rule: from two particular premises, no valid conclusion follows. This is because particular statements don't distribute the middle term, leaving both extremes independent.
questions.explanation_method_hi: दोनों particulars 'pens' term share करते हैं। 'कुछ pens जो pencils' और 'कुछ pens जो markers' pen-space में disjoint हो सकते हैं — pencil-marker conclusion block।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Classical rule: दो particular premises से valid conclusion नहीं — particulars middle term distribute नहीं करते।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All children are innocent. Some innocent people are wise. Conclusions: I. Some children are wise. II. All wise people are children.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी children innocent हैं। कुछ innocent लोग wise हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ children wise हैं। II. सभी wise लोग children हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is undistributed middle: 'some innocent who are wise' might be entirely outside the children subset of innocent people.
options.trap_reason_hi: I undistributed middle: 'कुछ innocent जो wise' children के बाहर हो सकते हैं।

options.label: B
options.trap_reason_en: II requires wise ⊆ children; nothing in premises forces that. Improper conversion of multiple steps.
options.trap_reason_hi: II के लिए wise ⊆ children चाहिए; premises नहीं देते।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: Both fail: I by undistributed middle, II by lack of premise support.
options.trap_reason_hi: दोनों fail: I undistributed middle से, II premise-support के बिना।

questions.explanation_method_en: Children ⊆ innocent. Some innocent are wise — but they could be exactly the non-children innocent people. So I not forced. II would require wise ⊆ children, never stated.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Universal subset + particular overlap with the supersetdoes NOT propagate to the subset (undistributed middle). Always check if the 'some' statement could be entirely outside the subset.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Children ⊆ innocent। 'कुछ innocent, wise' children के बाहर हो सकते हैं ⇒ I force नहीं। II के लिए wise ⊆ children चाहिए — premises नहीं।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Universal subset + superset पर particular overlap subset पर propagate नहीं होता (undistributed middle)।

questions.stem_en: Statements: No fish is a bird. All birds fly. Conclusions: I. No fish flies. II. Some flying things are not fish.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: कोई fish, bird नहीं है। सभी birds उड़ते हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कोई fish उड़ता नहीं है। II. कुछ उड़ने वाली चीज़ें fish नहीं हैं।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I claims no fish flies, but premises say 'no fish is a bird' and 'birds fly'. Fish could fly without being birds (consider mythical flying fish that aren't classed as birds).
options.trap_reason_hi: I overstates: 'कोई fish bird नहीं' और 'birds उड़ते हैं' से 'fish उड़ नहीं सकते' force नहीं — fish bird हुए बिना उड़ सकती हैं।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: II follows from existential import on birds (birds non-empty ⇒ some flyers are birds; birds aren't fish ⇒ some flyers aren't fish). I overstates the constraint.
options.trap_reason_hi: II existential import से valid; I overstates।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: II follows: birds exist (assumed non-empty) and they fly, and they're not fish ⇒ some flying things are not fish.
options.trap_reason_hi: II valid: birds (non-empty) उड़ते हैं और fish नहीं हैं ⇒ 'कुछ flying things, fish नहीं'।

questions.explanation_method_en: Birds ⊆ flying things; fish ∩ birds = ∅. Existential import: some flying things are birds (birds non-empty), and those are not fish ⇒ some flying things are not fish (II). I would require all flying things to be non-fish, which the premises don't establish.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Universal negatives don't propagate strongly. From 'no fish is a bird' and 'all birds fly', we get 'some flying things are not fish' (existential import) but NOT 'no fish flies' — the latter requires fish to be excluded from ALL flying things, not just birds.
questions.explanation_method_hi: Birds ⊆ flying; fish ∩ birds = ∅। Existential import: 'कुछ flying birds हैं' और birds, fish नहीं ⇒ 'कुछ flying, fish नहीं' (II)। I के लिए सभी flying things से fish excluded होने चाहिए — premises नहीं देते।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Universal negative strongly propagate नहीं होते। 'कोई fish, bird नहीं' और 'सभी birds उड़ते' से 'कुछ flying, fish नहीं' तो निकलता है पर 'कोई fish उड़ता नहीं' नहीं।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All artists are creative. Some creative people are scientists. Conclusions: I. Some artists are scientists. II. Either some artists are scientists OR no artist is a scientist.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी artists creative हैं। कुछ creative लोग scientists हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ artists scientists हैं। II. या तो कुछ artists scientists हैं, या कोई artist scientist नहीं है।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is undistributed middle and not forced. Premises allow artist-scientist overlap to be empty.
options.trap_reason_hi: I undistributed middle से force नहीं। Artist-scientist overlap empty हो सकता है।

options.label: C
options.trap_reason_en: II is the tautological 'either-or' covering all possibilities — always valid. I alone is not.
options.trap_reason_hi: II tautological 'either-or' है — हमेशा valid। I अकेला नहीं।

options.label: D
options.trap_reason_en: II is logically vacuous (covers all cases) but technically follows. I doesn't.
options.trap_reason_hi: II tautological पर technically valid; I नहीं।

questions.explanation_method_en: I depends on whether artist-scientist overlap is non-empty; premises don't decide. II (an 'either A or not-A' for the artist-scientist relation) is a tautology, always true. So II follows; I does not necessarily.
questions.explanation_concept_en: Complementary-pair conclusions ('either some A are B OR no A is B') are tautologies — they always follow because they exhaust the logical possibilities. They appear in exam questions to test whether students recognize the tautology.
questions.explanation_method_hi: I के लिए artist-scientist overlap non-empty चाहिए; premises silent। II ('या तो कुछ A, B हैं या कोई A, B नहीं') tautology — हमेशा valid।
questions.explanation_concept_hi: Complementary pairs ('या तो कुछ A, B हैं या कोई A, B नहीं') tautology होते हैं — हमेशा valid। Exam में इन्हें पहचानना है।

questions.stem_en: Statements: All managers are leaders. Some leaders are coaches. Conclusions: I. Some managers are coaches. II. No manager is a coach.
questions.stem_hi: कथन: सभी managers, leaders हैं। कुछ leaders, coaches हैं। निष्कर्ष: I. कुछ managers, coaches हैं। II. कोई manager coach नहीं है।

options.label: A
options.trap_reason_en: I is not forced (undistributed middle on 'leaders'). The 'some leaders who are coaches' might miss managers entirely.
